question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted ContractCallRequest with gas set exactly at or below GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10,000) so that scaleGas() returns 0, causing gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(0) to always succeed and completely bypass the gas-per-second throttle gate, allowing unlimited high-cost EVM transactions to be submitted and exhausting backend compute resources?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the gas field of a submitted transaction by setting it to the minimum allowed value (21,000 per ContractCallRequest @Min(21_000)) which after scaleGas() division by 10,000 yields only 2 tokens consumed, allowing the attacker to submit far more gas-heavy transactions than the gasPerSecond limit intends, effectively multiplying their allowed throughput by a factor of ~5000?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the per-second request rate limit enforced by rateLimitBucket (default 500 RPS) by distributing requests across multiple concurrent TCP connections or source IPs, since rateLimitBucket is a single shared in-memory Bucket with no per-IP or per-session partitioning, allowing one attacker to starve all legitimate users from the shared 500 RPS budget?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the refillGreedy(gasLimit, Duration.ofSeconds(1)) burst behavior of gasLimitBucket to submit a burst of maximum-gas transactions (up to gasPerSecond capacity) all within the first millisecond of each second, completely consuming the entire per-second gas budget before other users can submit any transactions, causing sustained denial of service for all other users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the refillGreedy strategy of rateLimitBucket, which allows the full capacity (500 tokens) to be consumed instantly at the start of each second, to send 500 crafted transactions in a single burst at second boundary, monopolizing the entire rate limit window and denying service to all other concurrent users for the remainder of that second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the opcodeRateLimitBucket (default 1 RPS, no SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED) by sending concurrent opcode requests that race on the non-synchronized bucket, potentially allowing multiple requests to pass tryConsume(1) simultaneously due to lack of thread-safety guarantees, bypassing the intended 1 RPS opcode limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the gas field of submitted transactions to a value just above GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (e.g., 10,001) so that scaleGas() returns 1 token consumed from gasLimitBucket regardless of actual EVM execution cost, allowing the attacker to submit transactions that consume far more real compute than the throttle accounts for, effectively defeating the gas-based rate limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a denial-of-service against all other users by sending exactly requestsPerSecond (500) crafted transactions per second continuously, since rateLimitBucket is shared globally with no per-user or per-IP quota, starving legitimate users who share the same global 500 RPS bucket?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with submitted transactions by setting gas to Long.MAX_VALUE or near-maximum long values, and if scaleGas() overflows or produces unexpected results via Math.floorDiv, cause the gasLimitBucket to consume an incorrect number of tokens, either bypassing the throttle or triggering an arithmetic exception that crashes the request handler?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restore() path in ThrottleManagerImpl by crafting transactions that always fail (e.g., reverting contracts), causing gasLimitToRestoreBaseline tokens to be added back to gasLimitBucket via addTokens(), and then immediately submitting new transactions before the refill cycle, effectively recycling gas budget faster than the 1-second refill window and exceeding the intended gasPerSecond limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the from field of a ContractCallRequest to a spoofed address (since no authentication is enforced on the from field), bypassing any RequestFilter rules that match on FilterField.FROM, and submitting transactions that would otherwise be REJECT-actioned, causing unauthorized transaction execution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket uses SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED while rateLimitBucket does not, creating a TOCTOU window where a request passes rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1) but then blocks on the synchronized gasLimitBucket, allowing an attacker to flood rateLimitBucket checks and cause thread contention or starvation on the synchronized gasLimitBucket monitor?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the data field of a ContractCallRequest to contain a specific byte sequence that matches a RequestFilter with FilterType.CONTAINS and ActionType.LOG, causing the server to log the full request payload (including potentially sensitive fields like from, to, value, data) at INFO level, leading to information disclosure through log aggregation systems?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user submit transactions with gas values that are multiples of GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10,000) to precisely control token consumption from gasLimitBucket, allowing them to calculate the exact remaining capacity of the shared gas bucket and time their attacks to exhaust it at precise intervals, denying service to other users in a coordinated manner?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the Accept-Encoding header to include 'gzip' while sending crafted transactionIdOrHash values to the opcodes endpoint, bypassing the header check and consuming the opcodeRateLimitBucket (1 RPS), effectively blocking all legitimate opcode trace requests for the entire second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of per-IP throttling in rateLimitBucket by using a botnet or distributed proxies to send requests from many IPs, each consuming tokens from the single shared rateLimitBucket, achieving a coordinated denial-of-service that exhausts the global 500 RPS limit while each individual IP appears to send only a few requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the gas field to submit transactions with gas=21000 (minimum allowed), causing scaleGas() to return 2 tokens, and then exploit the restore() mechanism after transaction failure to get gasLimitToRestoreBaseline tokens back (100% of 2 = 2), effectively making failed transactions cost zero net gas tokens from the bucket and allowing unlimited failed transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user craft ContractCallRequest payloads with extremely large data fields (hex-encoded bytecode) that pass the @Hex validation but cause the RequestFilter CONTAINS matching to perform expensive string operations on large strings, leading to CPU exhaustion in the filter evaluation loop before the throttle can reject the request?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the integer truncation in scaleGas() (Math.floorDiv(gas, 10_000)) by submitting transactions with gas values like 19,999 which scale to 1 token but represent nearly 2x the gas of a 10,001-gas transaction, allowing the attacker to submit transactions with nearly double the intended gas budget per token consumed from gasLimitBucket?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the block field of a ContractCallRequest (e.g., setting it to a historical block number) to bypass RequestFilter rules that only match FilterField.BLOCK with FilterType.EQUALS on 'latest', submitting transactions that would otherwise be throttled or rejected under the 'latest' block filter?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared gasLimitBucket (SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED) as a timing oracle by measuring response latency differences between requests that succeed vs. fail the gas throttle check, inferring the current token count in the bucket and deducing the transaction submission rate of other users, constituting a side-channel information leak?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the estimate field of a ContractCallRequest (setting estimate=true vs estimate=false) to selectively match or avoid RequestFilter rules that filter on FilterField.ESTIMATE, bypassing REJECT or THROTTLE actions intended to limit gas estimation calls while still consuming rateLimitBucket tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket.addTokens() in restore() has no upper bound cap enforced at the ThrottleConfiguration level, allowing an attacker who can trigger many concurrent failing transactions to cause addTokens() to be called multiple times in parallel, potentially overflowing the bucket beyond its configured capacity and granting subsequent requests free gas tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the opcodeRateLimitBucket's lack of SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED (unlike gasLimitBucket) by sending concurrent opcode requests from multiple threads, causing a race condition where multiple tryConsume(1) calls succeed simultaneously on the non-synchronized bucket, allowing more than 1 opcode request per second to be processed?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the to field of a ContractCallRequest to target a specific contract address that matches a RequestFilter with FilterField.TO and ActionType.THROTTLE, and then send requests at exactly the filter's rate limit, consuming both the global rateLimitBucket and the per-filter bucket simultaneously, causing double-throttle exhaustion and denying service to users targeting that contract?
